denial_pattern_sources: plan_exclusion_overrides_state_mandate: NCSL State Health Insurance Mandates Database 2024; documented in CA, NJ, IL enforcement actions
non_specialist_reviewer: MHPAEA Final Rule 2024 (29 CFR § 2590.712); Cal. Health & Safety Code § 1374.72
denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

We received your provider's request on 03/23/2026 for Romiplostim (Nplate) related to Immune thrombocytopenia, chronic (D69.3). After review, we cannot approve this request.

Our review applied Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin (CPB) criteria and InterQual review standards. The documentation submitted does not demonstrate that the service is medically necessary for this member at this time. Romiplostim (Nplate) is non-preferred; a formulary alternative must be tried for Immune thrombocytopenia, chronic (D69.3). A lower-tier agent is available without demonstrated failure of the preferred option.

Specifically, the information provided does not satisfy the utilization-management requirements associated with formulary tier dispute. The clinical notes reviewed did not include sufficient detail to confirm that plan criteria were met. The requesting provider may submit additional records, but the current file does not support approval.

If your provider wishes to discuss this determination, they may contact our physician review line. Any discussion must occur within the timeframe described in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, send a written request and supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification number and the service reference shown on your explanation of benefits.

Sincerely,

Aetna Medical Management

This determination was made by Dr. J. Smith, Medical Director.
clinical_context: The 68-year-old member has Immune thrombocytopenia, chronic (D69.3). He has been treated under commercial coverage with Romiplostim (Nplate) recommended as the next appropriate step. Platelets 18k despite steroids and IVIG trials. This directly contradicts the insurer's position that formulary tier dispute bars coverage: the chart documents dates, doses, and objective findings that were submitted with the original request submitted in Q1 2026 but are not reflected in the denial letter's rationale. Treating clinicians note ongoing functional impairment and avoidable harm if his care is delayed further.
